The Waimakariri's braided system is one of the most dynamic river environments on earth — what was a channel yesterday might be a gravel bar today. Your driver reads the water with practised eyes, threading between channels at thrilling speed. The gorge section is particularly spectacular, where bluffs of ancient greywacke rock tower above the turquoise water. This is the same river that Canterbury's early settlers had to ford on their journeys west — today you cross it at 80 kilometres per hour.

reviews.summary: The Waimakariri River Jet Boating adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Jet boat through the braided Waimakariri River gorge in the Canterbury foothills. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Hanmer Springs Bungy Jump
pois.short: Bungy jump 35 metres into the Waiau River gorge at Hanmer Springs.
pois.commentary: The Hanmer Springs Bungy is a 35-metre jump over the Waiau River gorge, surrounded by native bush and mountain scenery. It's operated near the Hanmer Springs thermal resort town. The relatively moderate height makes it popular as a first bungy experience.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Hanmer Springs Bungy, set in the beautiful Waiau River gorge surrounded by native bush and mountain scenery. At 35 metres, this jump is less extreme than the big Queenstown bungy sites, but don't let the height fool you — standing on the platform looking down at the rocky gorge and river below is still a genuine test of courage. The bridge spans the narrowest point of the gorge, and the natural amphitheatre means your screams echo beautifully off the canyon walls. Many people choose Hanmer for their first bungy because the setting is so beautiful and the height is manageable. Afterwards, the Hanmer Springs thermal pools are just minutes away — the perfect reward for your bravery.

reviews.summary: The Hanmer Springs Bungy Jump adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Bungy jump 35 metres into the Waiau River gorge at Hanmer Springs. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Waiau River Jet Boat Hanmer
pois.short: Jet boat through the Waiau River gorge near Hanmer Springs.
pois.commentary: Jet boating on the Waiau River near Hanmer Springs takes you through a scenic canyon of native bush and limestone bluffs. The 30-minute ride includes 360-degree spins and high-speed runs through shallow braided sections. It's a popular add-on to the Hanmer Springs thermal resort visit.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at the Waiau River gorge, where a jet boat is about to show you why New Zealand invented this incredible machine. Sir William Hamilton developed the jet boat right here in Canterbury to navigate the shallow braided rivers of the high country, and the Waiau is a perfect showcase for the technology. Your boat will skim across water just centimetres deep, spin through 360-degree turns, and race through narrow gaps in the limestone gorge walls. The native bush lining the banks is lush with ferns and beech trees, and the surrounding mountains frame it all perfectly. The whole experience takes about 30 minutes, but it packs in enough thrills to fuel your stories for years. And the Hanmer Springs thermal pools are just up the hill when you're done.

reviews.summary: The Waiau River Jet Boat Hanmer adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Jet boat through the Waiau River gorge near Hanmer Springs. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Surfing at Sumner & New Brighton
pois.short: Catch waves at Christchurch's best surf beaches — Sumner and New Brighton.
pois.commentary: Sumner and New Brighton are Christchurch's premier surf beaches, offering consistent beach breaks suitable for beginners through to experienced surfers. Surf schools operate year-round with board and wetsuit hire. The best swells come from the south and east, particularly in autumn and winter.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at one of Canterbury's best surf spots. The beaches of Sumner and New Brighton pick up swells from the Southern Ocean and Pacific, producing consistent waves that break over sandy bottoms — perfect for learning and fun for experienced surfers alike. The water is cool year-round, so a good wetsuit is essential, but that's part of the experience. Sumner Beach, tucked at the base of the Port Hills, offers a more sheltered break, while New Brighton's long, exposed beach catches bigger swells. Surf schools here will have you standing on your first wave within an hour — the sandy seabed is forgiving and the local instructors know every sandbar shift. After your session, Sumner village has excellent cafes for warming up with a flat white.

reviews.summary: The Surfing at Sumner & New Brighton adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Catch waves at Christchurch's best surf beaches — Sumner and New Brighton. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Arthur's Pass Alpine Adventures
pois.short: Explore dramatic alpine terrain and waterfalls in Arthur's Pass National Park.
pois.commentary: Arthur's Pass National Park straddles the Southern Alps with walks ranging from the easy Devils Punchbowl Falls track to challenging alpine routes like Avalanche Peak. The park is home to kea, the world's only alpine parrot. Ice climbing is available in winter on frozen waterfalls.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Arthur's Pass, the highest settlement in the Southern Alps and the gateway to some of Canterbury's most dramatic alpine scenery. The village sits at 739 metres, surrounded by peaks that soar above 2,000 metres, and the range of adventures here is extraordinary. Walk the short track to Devils Punchbowl Falls, where water plunges 131 metres down a cliff face, or tackle the legendary Avalanche Peak route for a challenging climb to the summit with panoramic views in every direction. In winter, the frozen waterfalls attract ice climbers from around the world. Keep your ears open for the unmistakable call of the kea — the world's only alpine parrot — cheeky, intelligent birds that will investigate anything left unattended. Arthur's Pass is where the Canterbury Plains meet the wild West Coast.

reviews.summary: The Arthur's Pass Alpine Adventures adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Explore dramatic alpine terrain and waterfalls in Arthur's Pass National Park. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Whale Watch Kaikoura
pois.short: See giant sperm whales year-round in Kaikoura's deep coastal waters.
pois.commentary: Whale Watch Kaikoura is New Zealand's only marine-based whale watching operation, offering year-round encounters with giant sperm whales. The Kaikoura Canyon, a 1,600-metre deep underwater trench close to shore, creates a nutrient-rich environment that attracts whales, dolphins, and seals. The company is Maori-owned and operated.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Kaikoura, one of the few places on earth where you can reliably see giant sperm whales year-round. Just offshore, the seafloor drops away into the Kaikoura Canyon — a massive underwater trench over 1,600 metres deep that comes remarkably close to land. This creates a nutrient superhighway that draws sperm whales, dusky dolphins, fur seals, and albatross in extraordinary numbers. Your boat will use hydrophones to locate the whales by their sonar clicks, then position you for the magical moment when a giant surfaces to breathe — these animals can be 18 metres long and weigh 50 tonnes. Whale Watch Kaikoura is Maori-owned and has operated since 1987, transforming what was a struggling fishing town into one of New Zealand's premier eco-tourism destinations.

reviews.summary: The Whale Watch Kaikoura adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. See giant sperm whales year-round in Kaikoura's deep coastal waters. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Dolphin Encounter Kaikoura
pois.short: Swim with pods of wild dusky dolphins in Kaikoura's coastal waters.
pois.commentary: Dolphin Encounter offers swimming and viewing experiences with pods of wild dusky dolphins off Kaikoura. Pods can number in the hundreds, and the dolphins are famously playful and interactive. Swimmers are provided with wetsuits and snorkels and encouraged to make sounds to attract the dolphins' curiosity.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at one of the world's best places to swim with wild dolphins. The waters off Kaikoura are home to large pods of dusky dolphins — acrobatic, curious, and wonderfully playful. You'll suit up in a wetsuit, slip into the water, and within moments you could be surrounded by dolphins spinning, leaping, and making eye contact just metres away. The trick is to make noise — sing, hum, squeal — the dolphins are drawn to interesting sounds and will come in close to investigate. Pods here can number in the hundreds, and the experience of being in the water as dolphins barrel-roll around you is genuinely life-changing. The dusky dolphin is one of the most acrobatic of all dolphin species, and Kaikoura's population is one of the most interactive anywhere on earth.

reviews.summary: The Dolphin Encounter Kaikoura adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Swim with pods of wild dusky dolphins in Kaikoura's coastal waters. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Akaroa Harbour Sailing
pois.short: Sail the volcanic Akaroa Harbour and spot the world's smallest dolphin.
pois.commentary: Sailing on Akaroa Harbour explores the flooded crater of an ancient volcano on Banks Peninsula. The harbour is home to Hector's dolphins, the world's smallest and rarest marine dolphin. The French colonial heritage of Akaroa village adds cultural depth to the adventure.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Akaroa Harbour, a stunning natural harbour formed inside the crater of an ancient volcano on Banks Peninsula. You're about to sail across waters that are home to one of the world's rarest marine creatures — Hector's dolphin, the smallest dolphin species on earth, found only in New Zealand. These distinctive dolphins with their rounded dorsal fins often play around boats in the harbour. As you sail, the dramatic volcanic cliffs of the old crater rim rise around you, and your skipper will share the fascinating story of Akaroa's French colonial history — this little town was nearly the capital of a French New Zealand. The sailing conditions in the harbour are sheltered and reliable, making this a perfect experience for sailors of all levels.

reviews.summary: The Akaroa Harbour Sailing adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Sail the volcanic Akaroa Harbour and spot the world's smallest dolphin. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Castle Hill / Kura Tawhiti Bouldering
pois.short: Free bouldering among surreal limestone formations the Dalai Lama called a spiritual centre.
pois.commentary: Castle Hill (Kura Tawhiti) features hundreds of limestone boulders scattered across an alpine basin, offering world-class bouldering. The site was described by the Dalai Lama as one of the spiritual centres of the universe. Over 1,000 boulder problems have been documented across all difficulty grades. Access is free.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Kura Tawhiti, also known as Castle Hill, one of the most extraordinary bouldering destinations on the planet. Hundreds of massive limestone formations rise from the tussock grass like the ruins of an ancient castle, sculpted by millions of years of wind and rain into shapes that seem almost designed for climbing. The Dalai Lama visited in 2002 and declared this a spiritual centre of the universe, and standing among these ancient stones, you might agree. Over 1,000 boulder problems have been documented here, ranging from beginner-friendly slabs to desperately hard overhangs. The rock is grippy limestone, and the setting — a wide alpine basin ringed by mountains — is simply magical. Bring a crash pad if you have one, but even just wandering among the boulders is a memorable experience. Access is completely free.

reviews.summary: The Castle Hill / Kura Tawhiti Bouldering adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Free bouldering among surreal limestone formations the Dalai Lama called a spiritual centre. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Dark Sky Stargazing - Mt John Observatory
pois.short: Stargaze at the world's largest Dark Sky Reserve from Mt John Observatory.
pois.commentary: The Aoraki Mackenzie International Dark Sky Reserve is the world's largest, and Mt John Observatory near Lake Tekapo offers guided stargazing tours using powerful telescopes. The reserve's minimal light pollution reveals the Milky Way, Magellanic Clouds, and thousands of stars invisible from cities.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at Mt John, home to New Zealand's premier astronomical observatory and the gateway to the largest Dark Sky Reserve in the world. The Aoraki Mackenzie region has some of the clearest, darkest skies on earth, and tonight you're going to see stars like you've never seen them before. Through powerful telescopes, your astronomers will show you the craters of the moon in breathtaking detail, the rings of Saturn, distant galaxies, and the glowing clouds of stellar nurseries where new stars are being born. But it's what you see with your naked eyes that will truly astound you — the Milky Way blazing across the sky so brightly it casts shadows, the Magellanic Clouds glowing like smudges of light, and shooting stars streaking through the darkness. The reserve's strict lighting controls mean virtually no artificial light interferes with the view. Looking up from Mt John on a clear night is as close to a spiritual experience as astronomy gets.

reviews.summary: The Dark Sky Stargazing - Mt John Observatory adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Stargaze at the world's largest Dark Sky Reserve from Mt John Observatory. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Tasman Glacier Lake Kayaking
pois.short: Kayak among icebergs on the terminal lake of New Zealand's largest glacier.
pois.commentary: Kayaking on Tasman Glacier Lake puts you among icebergs calved from the terminal face of the Tasman Glacier — New Zealand's longest at 23.5 km. The icebergs can be up to 200 years old, glowing shades of blue and white. Guided tours operate from Aoraki/Mount Cook National Park.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at one of the most extraordinary kayaking experiences in New Zealand. The terminal lake of the Tasman Glacier is filled with icebergs — chunks of ancient ice that have calved from the face of New Zealand's longest glacier. Your kayak will weave between these floating sculptures, some as large as houses, glowing in shades of brilliant blue and pristine white. The ice you're paddling past is up to 200 years old, compressed and shaped by its journey down the 23.5-kilometre glacier from the heights of the Southern Alps. Aoraki/Mount Cook, New Zealand's tallest mountain at 3,724 metres, towers above the scene. The lake itself only formed in the last few decades as the glacier retreated, making this a landscape that is quite literally brand new. Touch an iceberg as you paddle past — you're holding frozen history in your hand.

reviews.summary: The Tasman Glacier Lake Kayaking adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Kayak among icebergs on the terminal lake of New Zealand's largest glacier. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Heli-Skiing Mount Cook Region
pois.short: Heli-ski untouched powder runs in the shadow of Aoraki/Mount Cook.
pois.commentary: Heli-skiing in the Aoraki/Mount Cook and Mackenzie region accesses terrain that's impossible to reach any other way. Runs of 1,500+ vertical metres on untouched powder descend through glacial terrain with views of New Zealand's highest peak. Experienced intermediate to expert skiers only.
pois.audio_story: You have arrived at one of the most exclusive skiing experiences on earth. Heli-skiing in the Mount Cook region puts you on runs that no lift system could ever reach — vast, untouched powder fields descending through glacial terrain in the shadow of Aoraki/Mount Cook, New Zealand's tallest peak at 3,724 metres. Your helicopter will land you on a pristine snowfield high in the Southern Alps, and from there, you ski down — 1,500 vertical metres or more of untracked powder with nobody in sight. The terrain is extraordinary — wide open glacial bowls, spine lines between ridges, and couloirs that funnel you between rock walls. Your guides are among the most experienced in the world, reading the snow and terrain to find the safest and most exciting lines. This is skiing in its purest form — no lifts, no crowds, no grooming — just you, the mountain, and metre after metre of perfect snow.

reviews.summary: The Heli-Skiing Mount Cook Region adventure draws rave reviews for the combination of adrenaline and spectacular scenery. Heli-ski untouched powder runs in the shadow of Aoraki/Mount Cook. Reviewers praise the professionalism of the operators and say the experience is worth every dollar for the memories it creates.

pois.name: Black Estate Winery
pois.short: Organic North Canterbury winery with a celebrated restaurant — local food matched with estate wines.
pois.commentary: Black Estate sits on the slopes above the Waipara Valley, and the restaurant has become a destination in its own right. The menu changes daily based on what's available from local farms and the estate garden, and every dish is thoughtfully paired with estate wines. The Pinot Noir and Chardonnay from the Home vineyard are standouts. The views across the plains to the Southern Alps complete a remarkable experience.

reviews.summary: The restaurant is called one of the best vineyard dining experiences in NZ. The organic wines impress. Some find the location a bit remote, but most say that's part of the appeal.

pois.name: Pegasus Bay Winery & Restaurant
pois.short: Family-owned Waipara estate with an award-winning restaurant and lush garden — Riesling and Pinot Noir stars.
pois.commentary: The Donaldson family's Pegasus Bay is the complete wine-country experience: an award-winning restaurant serving seasonal produce in a lush garden setting, a cellar door with an extensive tasting range, and wines that consistently rank among Canterbury's best. The Riesling (from dry to botrytis-sweet) is exceptional, and the Prima Donna Pinot Noir is their flagship. Allow a full morning or afternoon for the whole experience.

reviews.summary: The garden setting and restaurant earn universal praise. The Riesling range is called world-class. Can get very busy on weekends — booking is essential.

pois.name: Greystone Wines
pois.short: Award-winning North Canterbury estate — 'Trust the Chef' four-course lunch with organic estate wines.
pois.commentary: Greystone's dramatic cellar door is built into the limestone hills above Waipara. The 'Trust the Chef' four-course lunch celebrates North Canterbury's bounty, expertly matched with estate wines. Winemaker Dom Maxwell crafts expressive Pinot Noir and Riesling from organic vineyards. The views across the valley are stunning, and the architecture — concrete, stone and glass — is a highlight in itself.

reviews.summary: The 'Trust the Chef' lunch is called a must-do Canterbury experience. The architecture and setting impress. Some find it pricey, but the total experience is considered excellent value.

pois.name: Tussock Hill Vineyard
pois.short: Port Hills vineyard just 15 minutes from central Christchurch — tastings with city and Alps views.
pois.commentary: Tussock Hill's greatest asset is its accessibility — just 15 minutes from central Christchurch on the Port Hills. The vineyard produces good Pinot Noir and Riesling, and the tasting experience offers panoramic views across the city to the Southern Alps. It's the easiest way to fit a vineyard visit into a Christchurch itinerary without committing to a full day trip to Waipara.

reviews.summary: The proximity to Christchurch and the views are the main draws. The wines are pleasant if not at the level of Waipara's best. A good option for time-poor visitors.

pois.name: Cassels Brewing Co
pois.short: Family-run Christchurch brewery with world-award-winning beers and a popular taproom restaurant.
pois.commentary: The Cassels family has been brewing in Christchurch since 2009 and has racked up multiple World Beer Awards. The Woolston brewery and taproom is a convivial space with a full restaurant serving dishes designed to pair with the beers. The Milk Stout is legendary, the APA is a crowd-pleaser, and the seasonal specials are consistently creative. Tours of the brewery are available on weekends.

reviews.summary: Beer lovers praise the quality and the family atmosphere. The Milk Stout is frequently called the best in NZ. Some find the Woolston location a bit industrial, but the taproom itself is welcoming.

pois.name: Three Boys Brewery
pois.short: Inventive Christchurch microbrewery — the Oyster Stout (brewed with Bluff oysters) is a Kiwi icon.
pois.commentary: Ralph Bungard's Three Boys Brewery has been a Christchurch institution since 2005. The Oyster Stout — brewed with real Bluff oysters — is one of New Zealand's most famous craft beers, but the Wheat, IPA and Pils are equally well-crafted. The tap room is small and unpretentious, and Ralph is often behind the bar sharing stories about the brewing process.

reviews.summary: The Oyster Stout is a must-try novelty that actually delivers on flavour. The whole range is well-crafted. The small tap room means limited seating.

pois.name: Christchurch Farmers' Market
pois.short: Riccarton's Saturday morning market — one of NZ's largest with 70+ stalls under the old oak trees.
pois.commentary: Every Saturday morning, the grounds of Riccarton House fill with over 70 stalls selling Canterbury's finest produce. Under the shade of historic oak trees, you'll find everything from organic vegetables and free-range meats to artisan cheeses, fresh bread and specialty preserves. The coffee and breakfast options are excellent. It's been running since 1997 and is deeply embedded in Christchurch life.

reviews.summary: One of NZ's best and largest farmers' markets. The setting under the oaks is beautiful. Gets very busy by 10am — arrive early for the best selection.

pois.name: Gatherings
pois.short: Christchurch's celebrated farm-to-table restaurant — seasonal organic produce and wild game.
pois.commentary: Gatherings champions Canterbury's organic producers and wild food. The menu changes with the seasons but always centres on organic vegetables, complemented by wild game, sustainably sourced seafood and ethical meats. The dining room is warm and contemporary, and the wine list features Canterbury and Central Otago producers. It's the restaurant that best represents Christchurch's growing farm-to-table movement.

reviews.summary: The seasonal approach and local sourcing earn consistent praise. The wild game dishes are highlights. Some find portion sizes modest for the price, but the quality is excellent.

pois.name: Inati
pois.short: 'To share' in te reo Māori — inventive sharing plates served around an open kitchen in central Christchurch.
pois.commentary: Named after the Māori word for 'to share,' Inati is built around communal dining and an open kitchen where you can watch every dish being prepared. The menu is designed for sharing — expect innovative plates that draw on Asian, Pacific and European influences, all using Canterbury produce. The atmosphere is convivial and the cooking is precise. The cocktail programme is equally creative.

reviews.summary: The open-kitchen concept creates a dynamic dining experience. The sharing format is fun for groups. Some solo diners find the format less suited to eating alone.

pois.name: Cellar Door Wine Bar
pois.short: Wine bar in the historic Christchurch Arts Centre — Canterbury and NZ wines in a Gothic Revival setting.
pois.commentary: Cellar Door occupies one of the most atmospheric spaces in Christchurch — within the neo-Gothic Arts Centre, originally the University of Canterbury. The curated wine list focuses on Canterbury and wider NZ, with many available by the glass. Cheese and charcuterie boards complement the wines. It's a sophisticated spot for an afternoon drink, especially after exploring the Arts Centre's galleries and shops.

reviews.summary: The heritage setting is the standout feature. The wine selection is well-curated. Some find it quiet on weekday afternoons, but the ambiance compensates.

pois.name: The Brewery
pois.short: Christchurch's central brewpub — house-brewed beers, shared plates and a post-quake success story.
pois.commentary: The Brewery emerged in the post-earthquake rebuild as one of Christchurch's most popular gathering spots. The house-brewed beers are well-crafted (the Hazy IPA and Czech Pilsner are standouts), and the kitchen serves shared plates designed to pair. The industrial-chic space opens onto a sunny courtyard, and the location in the rebuild precinct tells the city's resilience story.

reviews.summary: A welcome addition to post-quake Christchurch. The beers are solid and the food is good. The rebuild-precinct location is convenient.

pois.name: Akaroa French-Inspired Dining
pois.short: French heritage harbour town with excellent seafood dining — salmon, mussels and the famous Akaroa salmon.
pois.commentary: Akaroa's French colonial heritage lives on in its food scene. The harbour-side restaurants serve some of Canterbury's freshest seafood — Akaroa salmon (farmed in the harbour), green-lipped mussels and blue cod are highlights. Ma Maison, The Trading Rooms and Bully Hayes are among the best. The 90-minute drive from Christchurch over the Port Hills adds to the sense of discovery.

reviews.summary: The French-influenced food and harbour setting create a special atmosphere. The salmon is the freshest you'll find. The drive from Christchurch deters some, but rewards those who make the trip.

pois.name: Kaikōura Seafood BBQ
pois.short: Iconic roadside crayfish caravan north of Kaikōura — the freshest half-cray on the South Island.
pois.commentary: The Kaikōura Seafood BBQ caravan on the coastal road north of town has become one of New Zealand's most iconic food stops. Fresh crayfish (half or whole), battered fish, and seafood platters are served from a simple caravan with views across to the Kaikōura Ranges. It's unpretentious, delicious and quintessentially New Zealand. The half-cray with garlic butter is the classic order. Cash or card accepted.

reviews.summary: The freshest crayfish you'll find anywhere. The roadside setting adds to the experience. Simple and perfect — don't expect fine dining.

pois.name: Shamarra Alpaca Farm & Café
pois.short: Waipara alpaca farm with a café — meet alpacas, taste local food and browse the farm shop.
pois.commentary: Shamarra combines an alpaca farm experience with a café serving local Waipara produce. The kids get to meet and feed the alpacas while adults enjoy excellent coffee, cabinet food and the farm shop's local products (honey, preserves, artisan goods). It's a fun family detour when visiting the Waipara wine district and combines well with cellar door visits to nearby wineries.

reviews.summary: Great for families combining food and farm experiences. The café food is good. The alpacas are a hit with children.

pois.name: Kaikōura Peninsula Seal Colony
pois.short: Hundreds of NZ fur seals basking on the rocks at Point Kean — a 5-minute walk from Kaikōura town.
pois.commentary: The Kaikōura Peninsula walkway leads past a large colony of NZ fur seals (kekeno). The seals are remarkably tolerant of visitors but maintain a 10-metre distance. Pups play in rock pools during summer. The walkway continues around the peninsula with spectacular mountain-meets-sea views.

reviews.summary: Must-do in Kaikōura. Seals are incredibly photogenic. The peninsula walkway is excellent. Free.

pois.name: Dolphin Encounter Kaikōura
pois.short: Swim with hundreds of dusky dolphins in the wild — Kaikōura's iconic marine encounter.
pois.commentary: Dusky dolphins gather in pods of 100-1000 in Kaikōura's productive waters. You can swim among them — they're curious and playful, often performing acrobatics around snorkellers. Non-swimmer viewing trips are also available. Best from October to April.

reviews.summary: Unforgettable experience. The dolphins are incredibly interactive. Water is cold — wetsuits provided. Book early.

pois.name: Kaikōura Albatross Encounter
pois.short: Up-close encounters with albatross, petrels, and shearwaters attracted by natural chum on the Kaikōura shelf.
pois.commentary: Albatross Encounter takes you to the edge of the Kaikōura Canyon where fish oils and chum attract a remarkable gathering of pelagic seabirds. Giant petrels, Hutton's and Buller's shearwaters, and several albatross species come within metres of the boat.

reviews.summary: Incredible for birders. Up to 12 species of albatross and petrel. Bring a good camera. Seasickness possible.

pois.name: Orana Wildlife Park — NZ Conservation
pois.short: NZ's only open-range zoo — breeding programmes for kiwi, tuatara, and Canterbury's endangered species.
pois.commentary: Orana Park takes a conservation-first approach with successful breeding programmes for great spotted kiwi, tuatara, blue duck, and Canterbury gecko. The kiwi encounter lets you into the nocturnal house for close viewing. Also home to NZ's only gorilla troop.

reviews.summary: Excellent conservation focus. The kiwi encounter is outstanding. Great for families. Spacious grounds.

pois.name: Willowbank Wildlife Reserve — Kiwi House
pois.short: See kiwi up close in a purpose-built nocturnal house — one of the best kiwi viewing experiences in NZ.
pois.commentary: Willowbank's kiwi house is widely considered one of the best places to see kiwi in New Zealand. The reversed day-night cycle means birds are active when you visit. Also home to tuatara, kea, and endangered Canterbury species. The Ko Tāne cultural experience runs here at night.

reviews.summary: Best kiwi viewing experience many visitors have. The birds are very active and close. Combine with Ko Tāne.

pois.name: Akaroa Harbour — Hector's Dolphin Cruise
pois.short: The world's smallest and rarest dolphin — Hector's dolphins play in Akaroa Harbour year-round.
pois.commentary: Akaroa Harbour is one of the most reliable places to see Hector's dolphins, the world's smallest marine dolphin, found only in NZ. Their distinctive rounded dorsal fin makes them easy to identify. Black Cat Cruises and Fox II Sailing offer nature cruises with very high sighting rates.

reviews.summary: Hector's dolphins almost guaranteed. The harbour itself is stunning. Swimming with dolphins available in summer.

pois.name: Banks Peninsula — White-Flippered Penguin Colony
pois.short: The white-flippered penguin — a rare subspecies found only on Banks Peninsula — comes ashore at dusk.
pois.commentary: The white-flippered little blue penguin is found only on Banks Peninsula and Motunau Island. Pohatu Penguins at Flea Bay runs dusk viewing tours where you can watch these rare penguins waddle up to their nest boxes. The conservation project has boosted numbers from 600 to over 5,000 pairs.

reviews.summary: Incredible conservation success story. The penguins are adorable. Book ahead for the evening tour. Gravel road in.

pois.name: Arthur's Pass — Kea Encounters
pois.short: The world's only alpine parrot — cheeky kea roam Arthur's Pass village stealing sandwiches and dismantling cars.
pois.commentary: Arthur's Pass village is arguably the best place in NZ to see kea. These intelligent alpine parrots hang around car parks and the village, investigating (and often destroying) anything they can get their beaks on. Entertaining but remember — don't feed them.

reviews.summary: Kea are hilarious and fascinating. They will investigate your car. Great photo opportunities. Don't leave anything unsecured.

pois.name: Te Waihora / Lake Ellesmere — Wetland Birds
pois.short: Canterbury's vast coastal lagoon — NZ's most important site for black stilt and other wetland birds.
pois.commentary: Te Waihora is NZ's fifth-largest lake but only 2 metres deep, making it incredibly productive for birdlife. Over 160 species have been recorded here, including the critically endangered black stilt (kakī). The Greenpark Sands and Kaitorete Spit are best for birding.

reviews.summary: Outstanding birding but vast and exposed. Bring a scope. Best spots are Greenpark Sands and Kaitorete Spit.

pois.name: Motunau Beach — Penguin Colony
pois.short: White-flippered and little blue penguins nest along this quiet Canterbury coast — watch from the beach at dusk.
pois.commentary: Motunau Beach hosts both white-flippered and standard little blue penguins. The local community runs a predator-trapping programme that has significantly increased penguin numbers. Evening viewing from the beach is free — maintain distance and keep torches off.

reviews.summary: Quiet, uncrowded penguin viewing. Best at dusk during breeding season (Sep-Feb). Support the local trapping project.

pois.name: Ashburton Lakes — Black Stilt Habitat
pois.short: The braided rivers and alpine lakes of inland Canterbury — last wild stronghold of the critically endangered kakī (black stilt).
pois.commentary: The Ashburton Lakes and surrounding braided rivers are the only remaining wild breeding habitat for kakī, the world's rarest wading bird with fewer than 200 wild individuals. DOC's Kakī Recovery Programme operates from nearby Twizel. Sightings require patience and binoculars.

reviews.summary: A pilgrimage for birders. Kakī sightings not guaranteed but the braided river landscape is stunning regardless.

pois.name: Travis Wetland Nature Heritage Park
pois.short: Christchurch's largest freshwater wetland — bittern, fernbird, and pūkeko in the heart of the city.
pois.commentary: Travis Wetland is a 116-hectare freshwater wetland that survived Christchurch's urban expansion. Boardwalks loop through raupo swamp, sedgeland, and open water. Australasian bittern, spotless crake, and fernbird are present, plus abundant waterfowl.

reviews.summary: Excellent urban wetland. Well-maintained boardwalks. Good for families. Best early morning for bittern.

pois.name: Hinewai Reserve — Banks Peninsula Forest
pois.short: Hugh Wilson's remarkable native forest restoration — 1,230 hectares of regenerating bush teeming with birds.
pois.commentary: Hinewai Reserve is the life's work of botanist Hugh Wilson, who let 1,230 hectares of Banks Peninsula farmland regenerate to native forest. The result is extraordinary — bellbird, kererū, and kōkako-reintroduction candidates fill the bush. Walking tracks cross the reserve.

reviews.summary: Inspiring conservation story. The bush is dense and birdy. Tracks can be steep and muddy. Free to visit.

pois.name: Godley Head — Penguin Corridor
pois.short: Protected penguin corridor on the Christchurch coast — little blue penguins commute through here at dusk.
pois.commentary: Godley Head on the Lyttelton Harbour entrance has a protected penguin corridor where little blue penguins cross from the harbour to their nesting burrows. The DOC-managed site includes predator traps and monitoring. Watch from the designated viewing areas at dusk.

reviews.summary: Nice combined walk and penguin viewing. The harbour views are spectacular. Dusk visits essential for penguins.

pois.name: Rakaia River — Braided River Birds
pois.short: Canterbury's braided rivers host black-fronted tern, wrybill, and banded dotterel — unique NZ species found nowhere else.
pois.commentary: Canterbury's braided rivers are globally significant habitats. The Rakaia is home to black-fronted tern (tara), wrybill (ngutu parore), banded dotterel, and black stilt. These species evolved to breed on the shingle islands. 4WD access to good viewing spots.

reviews.summary: Unique birding experience. Bring binoculars and a spotting scope. Spring breeding season is best.

pois.name: Pegasus Bay — Seabird Watching
pois.short: Pelagic seabirds visible from shore — Hutton's shearwater, albatross, and petrel in the Canterbury Bight.
pois.commentary: Pegasus Bay and the Canterbury Bight offer some of NZ's best shore-based pelagic birding. Strong easterly winds push Hutton's shearwaters, various albatross species, and giant petrels close to shore. The Kaikōura coast is particularly productive.

reviews.summary: Requires patience and the right conditions. A scope is essential. Best in strong onshore winds.

pois.name: Twizel Kakī Recovery Centre
pois.short: DOC's breeding programme for the world's rarest wading bird — see kakī (black stilt) up close.
pois.commentary: The Kakī Recovery Centre near Twizel is where DOC breeds and rears kakī (black stilt) for release into the wild. With fewer than 200 in the wild, every chick matters. Guided visits offer a window into one of NZ's most critical conservation programmes.

reviews.summary: Fascinating and humbling conservation work. Small-group visits only. Call ahead to arrange.

pois.name: Christchurch Art Gallery Te Puna o Waiwhetū
pois.short: Christchurch's stunning public art gallery with a curved glass façade and outstanding NZ art collection.
pois.commentary: Christchurch Art Gallery Te Puna o Waiwhetū is one of New Zealand's leading public galleries, housed in a striking building with a dramatic curved glass and metal façade on Worcester Boulevard. The gallery holds over 7,000 works with particular strengths in Canterbury and New Zealand art, including significant holdings of Bill Hammond, Shane Cotton, and Rita Angus. After serving as the Civil Defence headquarters during the 2010-2011 earthquakes, the gallery reopened in 2015 and has become a symbol of Christchurch's cultural recovery. Admission to the permanent collection is free. The gallery presents an ambitious programme of temporary exhibitions and events. The rooftop sculpture terrace offers great city views. The ground-floor café is popular, and the gallery shop is excellent for design-focused gifts. Located in the heart of the cultural precinct near the Canterbury Museum and Botanic Gardens.

reviews.summary: A truly excellent gallery with a fantastic building and strong collection. The earthquake story adds a powerful layer to the experience. Free entry and a great café make it an easy recommendation.

pois.name: Christchurch Street Art Precinct
pois.short: Vibrant open-air gallery of world-class murals born from Christchurch's post-earthquake creative renaissance.
pois.commentary: Christchurch's street art scene emerged powerfully from the devastation of the 2010-2011 earthquakes, transforming blank walls and empty lots into a vast open-air gallery. Artists from around the world have contributed large-scale murals throughout the central city, curated largely through the Oi YOU! and Spectrum street art festivals. Notable works include pieces by ROA, Askew One, Owen Dippie, and local artists. The art ranges from photorealistic portraits to abstract works, with many pieces reflecting themes of resilience, loss, and renewal. The best concentration of murals is in the blocks around Tuam, St Asaph, and Manchester Streets in the east of the city centre. Watch NZ Street Art produces a helpful map and app. Allow 1-2 hours for a self-guided walking tour, or join a guided tour for deeper insights. New works continue to appear regularly, making each visit different.

reviews.summary: An incredible and moving experience seeing how art has transformed the earthquake-damaged city. Some truly world-class murals everywhere you look. Download the map app and allow plenty of time to explore.

pois.name: Isaac Theatre Royal
pois.short: Beautifully restored 1908 heritage theatre, Christchurch's premier venue for performing arts and events.
pois.commentary: The Isaac Theatre Royal is Christchurch's grandest performance venue, originally opened in 1908 and magnificently restored after suffering significant damage in the 2011 earthquake. The $40 million restoration preserved the theatre's ornate Edwardian baroque interior — all gilt plasterwork, rich red velvet, and classical columns — while adding modern seating, acoustics, and accessibility. The 1,280-seat auditorium hosts touring musicals, the Royal New Zealand Ballet, Christchurch Symphony Orchestra, comedy acts, and major concerts. The restoration is considered one of New Zealand's finest heritage building recoveries and won multiple architecture awards. Tours are occasionally available when no performances are scheduled. The theatre's survival and restoration became a powerful symbol of Christchurch's post-earthquake recovery. Located on Gloucester Street in the cultural heart of the city, surrounded by excellent dining options.

reviews.summary: The restoration is breathtaking — they've done an incredible job bringing this beautiful theatre back. Every seat feels intimate despite its size. A real triumph of Christchurch's rebuild.

pois.name: The Tannery Christchurch
pois.short: Boutique shopping and dining complex in a beautifully converted Victorian tannery building in Woolston.
pois.commentary: The Tannery is a unique boutique retail and dining precinct housed in the beautifully converted former Woolston Tannery in southeast Christchurch. The Victorian industrial buildings have been transformed into an intimate, European-inspired complex of over 50 independent retailers, artisan food producers, and restaurants arranged around cobbled courtyards. After the 2011 earthquakes destroyed much of Christchurch's central retail, The Tannery became a vital hub for displaced boutique businesses and has since grown into a destination in its own right. Shops include independent fashion designers, bookstores, specialty food stores, handcraft galleries, and a boutique cinema. The dining options range from artisan bakeries to craft beer bars and fine dining. The industrial heritage architecture creates a characterful atmosphere quite different from conventional shopping centres. Open daily, with Saturday mornings particularly lively.

reviews.summary: Such a lovely place to browse and eat — feels like a little European quarter. The independent shops are really interesting and the building conversion is beautifully done. A real Christchurch hidden gem.

pois.name: Lyttelton Farmers Market
pois.short: Award-winning Saturday farmers market in the characterful port town of Lyttelton, a Canterbury institution.
pois.commentary: The Lyttelton Farmers Market is Canterbury's most popular and atmospheric market, held every Saturday morning in the London Street area of this historic port town. Running since 2005, the market brings together local growers, artisan producers, bakers, and food vendors in a vibrant community gathering. Stalls offer fresh seasonal produce, artisan bread, organic vegetables, local cheeses, specialty coffee, and ready-to-eat treats from diverse cuisines. The market has won multiple awards and is credited with helping revitalise Lyttelton after the 2011 earthquakes. The surrounding town is worth exploring — its heritage buildings, quirky independent shops, bars, and strong artistic community give Lyttelton a bohemian character unique in Canterbury. Reach Lyttelton via the road tunnel from Christchurch (15 minutes) or, even better, walk or cycle over the Bridle Path for spectacular harbour views. Market runs 10am–1pm, rain or shine.

reviews.summary: The best market in the South Island, bar none. Amazing food stalls, great produce, and a wonderful community atmosphere. Combine with a wander around quirky Lyttelton for a perfect Saturday morning.

pois.name: Sumner Beach
pois.short: Christchurch's favourite seaside village with a sheltered sandy beach, surf breaks, and great cafés.
pois.commentary: Sumner is Christchurch's most beloved beach suburb, a charming seaside village tucked beneath the dramatic volcanic bluffs of the Port Hills at the southern end of Pegasus Bay. The main beach offers safe swimming in a sheltered cove, while surfers head to the breaks at the eastern end near Cave Rock (Te Ana o Hineraki), a striking sea-carved rock formation of great significance to Ngāi Tahu. The village strip along Nayland Street has excellent cafés, restaurants, and a craft brewery. The beach is backed by a grassy esplanade perfect for picnics. Sumner was significantly affected by the 2011 earthquakes but has rebuilt with renewed energy. The Coastal Pathway connects Sumner to New Brighton via a scenic cycling and walking route. The beach faces east, catching the morning sun. Summer weekends are busy — the village atmosphere makes it worth the crowds.

reviews.summary: Christchurch's best beach suburb with a lovely village feel. The cafés are great and Cave Rock adds drama to the scenery. The Coastal Pathway ride is a fantastic way to arrive.

pois.name: New Brighton Pier & Beach
pois.short: Christchurch's iconic 300-metre pier extending into the Pacific, with adjacent beach and award-winning library.
pois.commentary: New Brighton Pier is the only recreational pier in Australasia, extending 300 metres into the Pacific Ocean from Christchurch's eastern coast. Built in 1997, the pier offers spectacular views up and down the sweeping Canterbury coastline and is a popular spot for fishing, walking, and watching surfers below. The beach stretches for kilometres in both directions, offering reliable surf breaks that attract boardriders year-round. Adjacent to the pier, the award-winning New Brighton Library is an architectural landmark with a distinctive glass tower overlooking the sea. The wider New Brighton area is undergoing revitalisation, with new eateries and community spaces emerging. The beach is fully exposed to the Pacific, so conditions can be windy — but on calm days the long, empty stretches of sand are magnificent. The pier is particularly atmospheric at dawn and dusk. Connected to Sumner via the Coastal Pathway.

reviews.summary: Walking to the end of the pier is a great experience, especially on a clear day. The beach is huge and the surf can be excellent. The library building is a real architectural highlight too.

pois.name: Clark's Mill Historic Flour Mill
pois.short: Rare surviving water-powered flour mill from 1866, one of New Zealand's oldest industrial heritage buildings.
pois.commentary: Clark's Mill is a remarkably intact water-powered flour mill built in 1866 near Maheno in North Otago, on the boundary of the Canterbury region. It is one of the very few stone-built, water-powered mills surviving in New Zealand and is classified as a Category I historic place by Heritage New Zealand. The three-storey limestone building was constructed by Scottish immigrant Thomas Clark and operated as a flour mill until 1901, when cheaper roller-milled flour made small stone mills uneconomic. The building features original milling machinery including French burr millstones. The mill race and water wheel system are still visible. The site sits in a peaceful rural setting on the banks of the Kakanui River. While the interior is not always accessible, the exterior and surrounding site can be freely viewed. The mill represents an important chapter in New Zealand's early agricultural and industrial heritage. Check with Heritage New Zealand for any organised open days.

reviews.summary: A fascinating piece of early industrial heritage in a lovely river setting. The stone building is beautifully constructed and amazingly well preserved. Worth a detour for history enthusiasts.

pois.name: Waipara Hills Winery
pois.short: Waipara Valley winery known for aromatic whites and elegant Pinot Noir with a scenic cellar door.
pois.commentary: Waipara Hills is one of the leading wineries in Canterbury's Waipara Valley, a wine region gaining recognition for its distinctive terroir and ability to produce outstanding Pinot Noir, Riesling, and Gewurztraminer. The striking cellar door building, with its curved rammed-earth walls, sits among vineyards with views to the Teviotdale hills. Wine tastings guide you through the current vintage range, and the on-site cafe serves platters of local cheeses and produce paired with the wines. The Waipara Valley's warm, dry microclimate, sheltered by the Teviotdale hills from coastal breezes, creates ideal growing conditions. The winery is a 50-minute drive north of Christchurch on SH1, making it an easy day trip or a stop en route to Hanmer Springs or Kaikoura.

reviews.summary: Visitors enjoy the beautiful cellar door setting and quality wines, particularly the Pinot Noir and Riesling. The platters pair well with tastings. A relaxing stop in the Waipara Valley.

pois.name: Brew Academy Christchurch
pois.short: Hands-on craft beer brewing experience in Christchurch where you brew your own beer to take home.
pois.commentary: Brew Academy offers a unique hands-on brewing experience in Christchurch, where participants get to brew their own batch of craft beer under expert guidance. Sessions run for around 2.5 hours and cover the full brewing process from selecting grains and hops to mashing, boiling, and fermentation. You choose from a range of beer styles, and your finished beer is bottled and ready for collection about three weeks later. The experience is popular for corporate events, stag parties, and anyone curious about the craft beer process. The venue includes a tasting bar where you can sample the house range while your brew progresses. Located in the central city, it makes for an educational and entertaining afternoon activity. Bookings are essential and can be made online.

reviews.summary: A fun, educational experience that appeals to beer enthusiasts and beginners alike. The instructors are engaging and knowledgeable. Taking home your own labelled beer is a great souvenir.

pois.name: Pohatu Penguins Akaroa
pois.short: Conservation-focused penguin viewing at the largest white-flippered penguin colony on Banks Peninsula.
pois.commentary: Pohatu is home to the largest mainland colony of korora (white-flippered little blue penguins), a subspecies found only on Banks Peninsula and Motunau Island. The conservation project, run by a dedicated local couple since 1990, has helped the colony grow to over 5,000 penguins through predator control and habitat restoration. Evening tours during breeding season (August to February) offer the chance to see penguins returning to their nests at dusk. Day tours include a scenic 4WD journey along dramatic coastal cliffs, sea kayaking in Flea Bay, and visits to nesting boxes. The remote location at the far end of Banks Peninsula, about 90 minutes from Akaroa, adds to the sense of adventure. All proceeds support ongoing conservation work. Booking is essential.

reviews.summary: An outstanding wildlife experience with passionate guides deeply committed to penguin conservation. The remote coastal scenery is spectacular. Evening tours seeing penguins emerge from the sea are magical.

pois.name: Mt Hutt Heli-Skiing
pois.short: Heli-skiing in the Southern Alps backcountry near Mt Hutt with access to pristine untracked powder.
pois.commentary: Mt Hutt and the surrounding Rakaia Range offer some of the South Island's most accessible heli-skiing terrain, with operators running from Methven during the June to September ski season. Helicopters whisk skiers and snowboarders to untouched alpine bowls and faces ranging from 1,500 to 2,500 metres, well above the tree line across wide-open terrain. Runs vary from gentle glacier descents to steep couloirs, catering to intermediate through expert levels. Full safety briefings, avalanche equipment, and mountain guides are included. The terrain benefits from Canterbury's easterly weather patterns that deliver reliable snowfall. Multi-run packages offer the best value, and conditions are assessed daily. Methven township provides accommodation, dining, and après-ski options just 90 minutes from Christchurch.

reviews.summary: An exhilarating backcountry skiing experience with knowledgeable guides and stunning alpine scenery. The powder quality can be exceptional after fresh snowfall. Weather-dependent, so flexibility in scheduling helps.

pois.name: Kaiapoi Woollen Mills Heritage Site
pois.short: Heritage site of the once-famous Kaiapoi Woollen Manufacturing Company, a Canterbury industrial landmark.
pois.commentary: The Kaiapoi Woollen Manufacturing Company was established in 1878 and became one of New Zealand's most significant textile manufacturers, producing the famous Kaiapoi blankets and woollen goods that were exported worldwide. At its peak, the mill employed hundreds of workers and was the economic heart of the town. The original mill buildings were damaged in the 2010 Canterbury earthquake and subsequently demolished, but the heritage site along the Kaiapoi River preserves the industrial history through interpretation panels and remnant features. The adjacent riverside walk provides a pleasant stroll past the site. The Kaiapoi Museum in the town centre holds a collection of mill artefacts and products. The town itself has undergone significant post-earthquake regeneration with new public spaces and the restored Kaiapoi River connection.

reviews.summary: An interesting stop for industrial heritage enthusiasts, though the actual mill buildings are gone. The interpretation panels tell the story well. Best combined with a walk along the Kaiapoi River.

pois.name: Akaroa Head Lighthouse
pois.short: Historic lighthouse at the entrance to Akaroa Harbour with a scenic coastal walk and dramatic views.
pois.commentary: Akaroa Head Lighthouse stands at the eastern headland guarding the entrance to Akaroa Harbour on Banks Peninsula. The current concrete lighthouse was built in 1980, replacing earlier structures dating back to 1880. The walk to the lighthouse from the road end takes about 45 minutes each way along an exposed coastal track with spectacular views of the harbour entrance, the Pacific Ocean, and on clear days, the Kaikoura Ranges to the north. Hector's dolphins are frequently spotted in the waters below, and seabirds including albatross soar along the cliffs. The track passes through farmland, so respect gate closures and livestock. The road to the trailhead from Akaroa passes through picturesque bays. Best visited on a calm, clear day for the views and to avoid exposed wind.

reviews.summary: A rewarding coastal walk with dramatic cliff views and excellent chances of spotting Hector's dolphins below. The lighthouse itself is modest but the setting is magnificent. Can be very windy.

pois.name: Lewis Pass Scenic Drive
pois.short: Alpine highway crossing between Canterbury and the West Coast through beech forest with hot springs nearby.
pois.commentary: State Highway 7 over Lewis Pass is the northernmost of the three main South Island alpine crossings, connecting Canterbury to the West Coast via Springs Junction. At 864 metres, it is lower than Arthur's Pass, making it a reliable year-round route, though chains may be required in winter. The drive passes through magnificent beech forest in the Lewis Pass National Reserve, with several short walks accessible from roadside car parks. The Maruia Springs hot pools, a natural thermal area now developed as a resort, sit just west of the pass. Further east, Hanmer Springs offers more extensive thermal bathing. The route is less travelled than Arthur's Pass, providing a quieter, more contemplative journey. Allow around three hours for the drive between Christchurch and Reefton, with stops.

reviews.summary: A beautiful and less-crowded alternative to Arthur's Pass with stunning beech forests. The hot springs stops add a relaxing element. The road is well-maintained but winding in sections.

pois.name: Hanmer Springs Thermal Pools
pois.short: Alpine thermal pools complex surrounded by mountain forests, 90 minutes north of Christchurch.
pois.commentary: Hanmer Springs Thermal Pools & Spa is the South Island's most popular thermal bathing destination, set in a picturesque alpine village surrounded by mountains and conifer forests. The complex features a series of open-air thermal pools at varying temperatures, sulphur pools, a freshwater pool, hydroslides, and a lazy river. The private thermal pool area offers a more serene experience. The water is naturally heated and rich in minerals, providing a relaxing soak with views to the surrounding mountain landscape. The adjacent day spa offers massage and beauty treatments. Hanmer Springs village has developed into a resort town with restaurants, cafes, boutique shopping, and adventure activities including bungy jumping, jet boating, and forest walks. The drive from Christchurch takes about 90 minutes through the Waipara wine region and across the Weka Pass. Evening soaking under stars is particularly magical in winter.

reviews.summary: A wonderful thermal bathing experience in a beautiful alpine setting. The variety of pools suits all preferences. The village has plenty to offer beyond the pools for a full day or weekend visit.

pois.name: Lake Tekapo Dark Sky Experience
pois.short: World-renowned dark sky reserve with guided stargazing tours at Mt John Observatory, Lake Tekapo.
pois.commentary: The Aoraki Mackenzie International Dark Sky Reserve, centred on Lake Tekapo, is one of only a handful of Gold-rated dark sky reserves in the world. The exceptionally clear, dark skies of the Mackenzie Basin provide some of the best astronomical viewing conditions on Earth. Guided stargazing tours at the Mt John University Observatory use research-grade telescopes to view planets, star clusters, nebulae, and galaxies, with expert astronomers providing commentary. On clear nights, the Milky Way arches across the sky with breathtaking clarity, and the Southern Cross, Magellanic Clouds, and other Southern Hemisphere features are spectacular. Tours operate year-round but winter offers the longest dark hours. Daytime visits to Mt John summit provide panoramic views across the turquoise lake and surrounding mountains. The Church of the Good Shepherd on the lake shore is one of New Zealand's most photographed landmarks. Book stargazing tours in advance as they sell out regularly.

reviews.summary: An awe-inspiring experience seeing the night sky in pristine conditions. The guides are expert astronomers who make the experience accessible and fascinating. Weather dependent but when clear, it is unforgettable.

pois.name: Akaroa French Heritage Village
pois.short: Charming historic harbour village with French colonial heritage, Hector's dolphins, and Banks Peninsula walks.
pois.commentary: Akaroa is New Zealand's only French-settled town, founded in 1840 when French colonists arrived just days after the British had claimed sovereignty. The charming harbour village retains its French character in street names (Rue Lavaud, Rue Jolie), colonial cottages, and a Francophile spirit that infuses local dining and culture. The Akaroa Museum tells the settlement's bicultural French-British story. The harbour is home to a resident population of Hector's dolphins, the world's smallest and rarest marine dolphin, viewable from harbour cruises and kayak tours. The Giant's House, a spectacular mosaic garden and sculpture park, is a highlight. Banks Peninsula's volcanic landscape offers walking tracks with dramatic coastal views, including the Banks Track multi-day hike. The drive from Christchurch takes 75 minutes over the volcanic hills, with summit views of the harbour's drowned volcanic crater. A beloved Canterbury day trip or weekend escape.

reviews.summary: A delightful village with genuine French heritage charm and stunning harbour setting. The Hector's dolphin encounters are special. The drive over the hill is scenic and the peninsula walks are excellent.

pois.name: Arthur's Pass Village & National Park
pois.short: Alpine village at the heart of Arthur's Pass National Park with walks, waterfalls, and cheeky kea parrots.
pois.commentary: Arthur's Pass village sits at 737 metres altitude in the heart of Arthur's Pass National Park, the gateway to some of the South Island's most dramatic alpine landscapes. The village serves as a base for walks ranging from the 30-minute Devil's Punchbowl waterfall track to multi-day alpine crossings. The national park spans the main divide of the Southern Alps, with beech forest, alpine herb fields, scree slopes, and glacial valleys. The cheeky kea (mountain parrot) is frequently encountered around the village, investigating cars and backpacks with intelligent curiosity. Short walks include Bridal Veil Falls, Dobson Nature Walk, and the Temple Basin access road for mountain views. The TranzAlpine train stops at the village, making it accessible without a car. The DOC visitor centre provides track information and weather updates. The village has basic accommodation, a cafe, and a general store. Arthur's Pass is on SH73 between Christchurch and Greymouth, about two hours from either city.

reviews.summary: A magnificent alpine setting with excellent walking tracks for all abilities. The kea encounters are entertaining. The Devil's Punchbowl waterfall is spectacular after rain.

pois.name: Akaroa Head Lighthouse
pois.short: Historic lighthouse at the entrance to Akaroa Harbour on Banks Peninsula.
pois.commentary: The Akaroa Head Lighthouse stands at the southeastern entrance to Akaroa Harbour, guiding vessels through the narrow harbour entrance since 1880. The current lighthouse replaced an earlier structure and commands dramatic views across the harbour and out to the open Pacific. A walking track from the road end leads to the lighthouse through farmland, taking about thirty minutes each way. The headland is an excellent vantage point for spotting Hector's dolphins, which frequent the harbour entrance year-round. The area around the lighthouse is exposed to wind, so dress warmly. The drive to the trailhead passes through the scenic volcanic landscape of Banks Peninsula. Combine with a visit to Akaroa village for a full day excursion from Christchurch.

reviews.summary: A pleasant walk to a historic lighthouse with great views. Good chance of seeing Hector's dolphins. Can be very windy.

pois.name: Christchurch Arts Centre / Te Matatiki Toi Ora
pois.short: A stunning Gothic Revival complex that served as the University of Canterbury for over a century, now home to galleries, studios, cinemas, and markets.
pois.commentary: The Arts Centre occupies a magnificent cluster of neo-Gothic stone buildings dating from 1877, originally built for Canterbury University College. After the university relocated in the 1970s, the precinct transformed into a vibrant cultural hub. Severely damaged in the 2011 earthquakes, it has been painstakingly restored over more than a decade. Today its courtyards host weekend markets, boutique cinemas, artist studios, and the beloved Great Hall — one of Christchurch's most photographed interiors. Ernest Rutherford, who split the atom, studied physics in the very room that now bears his name.

reviews.summary: Visitors are impressed by the beauty of the restored Gothic buildings and the quality of the weekend markets. Some note that not all buildings are fully reopened yet, but the overall atmosphere and history make it a must-visit.

pois.name: Christchurch Street Art Trail
pois.short: An open-air gallery of large-scale murals that emerged from the blank canvases left by Christchurch's earthquake demolitions.
pois.commentary: After the devastating 2010-2011 earthquakes levelled much of central Christchurch, the newly exposed walls and empty lots became canvases for local and international street artists. What began as guerrilla art evolved into a celebrated urban art scene, with over 60 major murals scattered across the central city. Highlights include works by Wongi Wilson, Rone, and Owen Dippie. The trail is free, self-guided, and constantly evolving — new pieces appear regularly while older ones are sometimes lost to reconstruction. It's one of Christchurch's most compelling stories of creative resilience.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the variety and scale of the murals. Many say it's the best free activity in Christchurch. Some note you need a map or app to find all the pieces, and some favourites have been lost to rebuilding.

pois.name: Castle Hill / Kura Tāwhiti
pois.short: A surreal landscape of massive limestone boulders scattered across alpine grassland — world-class bouldering and a place of spiritual significance to Ngāi Tahu.
pois.commentary: Castle Hill is an extraordinary natural amphitheatre of house-sized limestone outcrops that have been eroded into fantastical shapes over millennia. The Dalai Lama reportedly called it 'one of the true spiritual centres of the universe' during a 2002 visit. For climbers, it's one of the Southern Hemisphere's premier bouldering destinations, with hundreds of named problems across all grades. For everyone else, it's a captivating place to wander among the grey monoliths with the snow-capped Craigieburn Range as a backdrop. The site is a registered archaeological area — Ngāi Tahu Māori used it as a seasonal shelter for centuries.

reviews.summary: Climbers rate it world-class. Non-climbers enjoy the otherworldly scenery and easy access from the road. Can be very exposed and cold in winter — bring layers. Facilities are basic.

pois.name: Akaroa Historic French Quarter
pois.short: The only French colonial settlement in New Zealand, where street names, cottage architecture, and the tricolour still hint at an 1840 attempt to claim the peninsula for France.
pois.commentary: In 1840, a shipload of 63 French settlers arrived at Akaroa to establish a colony, only to find the British had raised the Union Jack just days earlier. The settlers stayed anyway, and their legacy threads through the town's character to this day — Rue Lavaud, Rue Jolie, the old French cemetery on L'Aube Hill, and the charming Langlois-Eteveneaux Cottage, now a small museum. A self-guided heritage walk passes more than 30 historic sites. The combination of French flavour, volcanic harbour scenery, and boutique food scene makes Akaroa one of Canterbury's most charming day trips.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the unique French-colonial atmosphere and the beautiful harbour setting. The heritage walk is well-signposted and manageable. Some say the town is overly touristy in peak summer.

pois.name: Quail Island / Ōtamahua
pois.short: A predator-free island sanctuary in Lyttelton Harbour with walking tracks, native revegetation, and fascinating layers of history from leper colony to Antarctic staging post.
pois.commentary: Quail Island sits quietly in Lyttelton Harbour, reached by a short boat trip from the wharf. Its history is remarkably layered — it served as a Māori seasonal camp, a quarantine station, a leper colony in the 1900s, and a staging post where Robert Falcon Scott and Ernest Shackleton exercised their dogs and ponies before heading to Antarctica. Today it's a DOC reserve undergoing native bush restoration, with several well-marked walking tracks looping through regenerating bush and past historic ruins. Picnic spots offer superb harbour views. It's a wonderfully peaceful escape just minutes from Christchurch.

reviews.summary: Visitors enjoy the tranquillity and the surprisingly rich history. The walks are gentle and suitable for families. Some note limited boat schedules and the need to bring your own food and water.

pois.name: Geraldine Vintage Car & Machinery Museum
pois.short: A quirky rural museum crammed with vintage cars, tractors, a biplane, and all manner of mechanical curiosities — a labour of love by local enthusiasts.
pois.commentary: Tucked away in the pleasant South Canterbury town of Geraldine, this volunteer-run museum is a treasure trove for anyone who appreciates old machinery. The collection spans early automobiles, veteran tractors, a de Havilland Tiger Moth biplane, and an astonishing array of engines, tools, and rural equipment that tells the story of how Canterbury's farms and communities were built. The volunteers are passionate and knowledgeable, often sharing personal stories connected to the exhibits. It's the kind of small-town museum that surprises visitors with its depth and charm.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently praise the helpful volunteers and the sheer volume of exhibits. Great for car enthusiasts and kids alike. Some say it needs better signage but that adds to its authentic, slightly chaotic charm.

pois.name: Hooker Valley Track
pois.short: One of New Zealand's most spectacular short walks — three swing bridges lead to a glacier-terminal lake directly beneath the face of Aoraki/Mount Cook.
pois.commentary: The Hooker Valley Track is arguably the most rewarding half-day walk in the country relative to its modest difficulty. The well-formed track crosses three dramatic swing bridges over milky-blue glacier-fed rivers, passes the Alpine Memorial commemorating climbers lost on the mountain, and ends at Hooker Lake, where icebergs from the Mueller and Hooker Glaciers float beneath the towering south face of Aoraki/Mount Cook. On a clear day, the views are utterly breathtaking. The track is largely flat and suitable for reasonably fit visitors of all ages, though it can be very exposed to weather — conditions change rapidly in the Mackenzie high country.

reviews.summary: Consistently rated one of the best walks in New Zealand. The swing bridges and glacier lake are highlights. Can be very busy in summer — early morning starts recommended. Weather can turn quickly; bring warm layers.

pois.name: Lyttelton / Ōhinehou
pois.short: Christchurch's quirky port town on the other side of the hills — a vibrant creative community with excellent cafes, the famous Saturday farmers' market, and harbour-front character.
pois.commentary: Lyttelton has always been Christchurch's gateway — it's where the Canterbury Association settlers first stepped ashore in 1850, and where goods and people have flowed through for nearly two centuries. Nestled in the volcanic caldera of Banks Peninsula, the town punches well above its weight culturally. Its Saturday Farmers' Market is one of the best in the South Island, and its cafes, bars, and restaurants are destination-worthy. Post-earthquake rebuilding has blended heritage character with contemporary creativity. The harbour views, the quirky shops along London Street, and the strong community spirit make Lyttelton one of Christchurch's best day trips.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the Saturday market, the creative atmosphere, and the harbour views. Cafes and restaurants are praised. Some note the steep drive through the tunnel or over the Port Hills. Parking can be difficult on market days.

pois.name: Mt John Observatory & Dark Sky Reserve
pois.short: The premier stargazing destination in the Southern Hemisphere, sitting within the Aoraki Mackenzie International Dark Sky Reserve — the world's largest.
pois.commentary: Perched above the shores of Lake Tekapo at 1,029 metres, Mt John Observatory has been home to the University of Canterbury's astronomical research since 1965. The Aoraki Mackenzie Dark Sky Reserve, established in 2012, is the largest dark sky reserve on Earth, and the clarity of the night sky here is extraordinary. Guided stargazing tours take visitors through the southern constellations, the Milky Way, and views through powerful telescopes. Even by day, the summit walk offers some of the best panoramic views in the Mackenzie Country — turquoise lakes, golden tussock, and the Southern Alps stretched across the horizon.

reviews.summary: Stargazing tours are described as magical and educational. The daytime walk is also highly rated for its views. Tours can be cancelled due to cloud — check conditions. It's cold at altitude, even in summer; rug up.

pois.name: Cave Stream Scenic Reserve
pois.short: Wade through a 594-metre limestone cave with a subterranean stream running through it — a genuine adventure that requires a torch, steady nerves, and warm clothing.
pois.commentary: Cave Stream is one of the most accessible wild caving experiences in New Zealand. The 594-metre-long cave was carved by a stream through Castle Hill limestone over thousands of years. You enter at the downstream end and wade, scramble, and squeeze your way upstream through the darkness, with the icy stream running between knee and waist deep for much of the journey. A small waterfall inside the cave requires climbing a short ladder. It's not for the claustrophobic, and DOC strongly recommends going with experienced companions, carrying multiple light sources, and wearing warm clothing and sturdy footwear. The reward is an unforgettable underground adventure.

reviews.summary: Described as thrilling and memorable. The water is very cold — wetsuits or thermals recommended. Not suitable for young children. Can be dangerous in high water; always check conditions at the information panel before entering.

pois.name: Willowbank Wildlife Reserve
pois.short: An intimate wildlife reserve where you can see kiwi in a natural nocturnal house and experience Ko Tane, an authentic pre-European Maori cultural performance.
pois.commentary: Willowbank combines native wildlife conservation with Maori cultural experience in a setting that feels more personal than a large zoo. The highlight for many visitors is the kiwi encounter — the nocturnal house is one of the best in the country for close-up viewing of these shy birds. The reserve also houses tuatara, kea, kaka, and other native species. In the evenings, Ko Tane offers a Maori cultural experience that includes a powhiri (welcome), hangi (earth-cooked feast), and traditional performance, set in a recreated pre-European village. The combination of wildlife and culture in one venue is unique.

reviews.summary: The kiwi viewing is praised as the best in the South Island. Ko Tane receives excellent reviews for its authenticity and warmth. Some note the general animal areas feel dated, but the native section and cultural experience more than compensate.

pois.name: International Antarctic Centre
pois.short: An immersive attraction telling the story of Antarctica and Christchurch's role as a gateway to the ice — including a storm simulation, Hagglund ride, and little blue penguins.
pois.commentary: Christchurch has been a launching point for Antarctic expeditions since the heroic age of Scott and Shackleton, and the International Antarctic Centre brings that connection to life. The indoor Antarctic Storm chamber drops to minus-18 degrees with wind chill, giving visitors a visceral taste of polar conditions. You can ride a Hagglund all-terrain vehicle over a challenging outdoor course, and the little blue penguin encounter features rescued birds that cannot survive in the wild. The exhibits cover Antarctic science, exploration history, and climate change. Located next to Christchurch Airport, it's perfectly positioned for a visit on arrival or departure day.

reviews.summary: Families praise the interactive experiences, especially the storm room and Hagglund ride. The penguin encounter is a favourite. Some adults find it somewhat geared toward children, but the history sections have depth.

pois.name: The Tannery / Christchurch Design Precinct
pois.short: A beautifully restored Victorian tannery transformed into Christchurch's premier boutique shopping and design precinct, with independent fashion, homewares, and dining.
pois.commentary: The Tannery occupies the beautifully restored former Woolston Tannery in southeast Christchurch — a Victorian industrial building that has been sympathetically converted into an elegant retail and dining precinct. After the earthquakes devastated the CBD, The Tannery became a refuge for independent retailers and designers who lost their city-centre shops. The result is a curated collection of New Zealand fashion, art, jewellery, and homeware stores alongside excellent cafes and restaurants. The architecture is a striking blend of original brick and iron industrial character with contemporary glass and steel additions.

reviews.summary: Shoppers appreciate the quality of the boutique stores and the beautiful building. The dining options are praised. Some find it quiet on weekdays and note it's not in the main tourist area.

pois.name: Rakaia Gorge
pois.short: A stunning river gorge where the turquoise Rakaia River squeezes through a narrow canyon — a hub for jet-boating, salmon fishing, and scenic walks.
pois.commentary: The Rakaia is one of Canterbury's great braided rivers, and where it carves through the foothills of the Southern Alps, it creates a dramatic gorge of blue-grey rock and impossibly turquoise water. The historic bridge over the gorge offers vertiginous views, and jet boat operators run exhilarating trips through the narrowest sections. The Rakaia is also one of the best salmon rivers in New Zealand — the annual salmon run draws anglers from around the country. A network of walking and mountain bike tracks explores the surrounding farmland and bush. The gorge is about an hour's drive from Christchurch.

reviews.summary: The jet boat rides and the turquoise water colour are highlights. Good picnic spots by the river. The gorge walk is short but scenic. Salmon fishing is seasonal — check regulations.

pois.name: Kaikoura Peninsula Walkway
pois.short: A spectacular coastal walkway around the Kaikoura Peninsula, where seal colonies bask on the rocks, mountains plunge toward the sea, and seabirds wheel overhead.
pois.commentary: The Kaikoura Peninsula Walkway is one of the most rewarding short walks in the South Island. The full loop takes about three hours and traces the rocky coastline around the peninsula, passing tidal rock pools, limestone formations, and large colonies of New Zealand fur seals — you'll walk within metres of them. The backdrop is extraordinary: the snow-capped Seaward Kaikoura Range rises directly behind the town to over 2,600 metres, creating one of the most dramatic mountain-meets-sea landscapes anywhere. Interpretive panels share the area's rich Maori and European history. Go at low tide for the best rock pool exploring.
